title: Race, Gender, and the Labor Market: Inequalities at Work By Robert L. Kaufman Lynne Reinner Publishers. 2010. 277 pages. $62.50 cloth
abstract: Robert Kaufman, a most prolific and dedicated scholar, has developed important theoretical perspectives and innovative methodological approaches for studies in the sociology of the labor market. Race, Gender, and the Labor Market: Inequalities at Work is Kaufman's magnum opus. It provides broad and synthetic, yet fine-tuned and nuanced, explanations of the practices that create and perpetuate racial and sexual inequality in the American labor market. Drawing on the queuing and devaluation perspectives, Kaufman successfully elaborates an integrated theoretical framework that grasps the allocation processes in the labor market at both the individual and job level. Applying these same integrated models, he accounts for both job segregation and earnings disparity across racial and sexual divides. The book begins with a discussion of the relative contributions to earnings inequality of three factors: composition (e.g., educational attainment), job segregation and differentiated return (i.e., unequal payout for the same labor input). In Chapter 2, Kaufman critically reviews existing theories representing both supply-side perspectives (e.g., human capital theory and worker preference approaches) and demand-side perspectives (e.g., Becker's competitive theory of discrimination and segmented labor market theories). Students of labor markets will find Kaufman's comprehensive and scrupulous review particularly useful. Next, he presents an integrated queuing perspective that synthesizes four structural factors: (1. the human-capital perspective regarding education, productivity and family status; (2. segmented market theory regarding job and firm characteristics; (3. the devaluation perspective regarding the racial and gender typing of job tasks; and (4. the power of market players to achieve their goals, and the incentives that drive them. In Kaufman's two-level model, individual workers queue up for jobs according to their human capital and preferences, and labor market positions (i.e., jobs) are queued as well. The factors that determine the relative position of jobs in the queue are general skill requirements, the product market structure, race-and-gender-stereotyped tasks, rates and growth of employment, and other structural elements such as unions and the public sector.
